title: Fever With Compensation Failing
summary: A 7-year-old with fever, poor intake, and lethargy is being monitored during transport. Identify the earliest meaningful deterioration point.
etiology_question: What is the most likely pattern?
response_question: What is the best immediate EMS response?

response_choices.label: Support oxygenation/perfusion, reassess frequently, and expedite transport
Observe only because SpO2 is initially normal
Delay transport until the fever resolves

feedback.timeline: The deterioration begins when tachycardia and tachypnea accelerate while systolic pressure starts falling.
feedback.etiology: Fever plus worsening perfusion trend is more concerning for sepsis with shock than simple fever.
feedback.response: The trend calls for oxygenation/perfusion support, frequent reassessment, and expedited transport.

hint: Look for the first point where more than one channel starts moving in the wrong direction, not just the most dramatic final value.

title: Asthma Fatigue
summary: A 10-year-old with asthma is initially working hard to breathe. Watch for the point where compensation begins to fail.

etiology_choices.label: Improving asthma because RR is falling

response_question: What is the best immediate EMS response?

response_choices.label: Escalate respiratory support and prepare for assisted ventilations
Reassure only because the respiratory rate is lower
Give oral fluids and continue routine monitoring

feedback.timeline: The danger point is when SpO2 keeps falling while RR drops after severe tachypnea; that can mean fatigue, not improvement.
feedback.etiology: A falling respiratory rate with worsening oxygenation is respiratory failure risk.
feedback.response: Escalate respiratory support and be ready to assist ventilations.

hint: Look for the first point where more than one channel starts moving in the wrong direction, not just the most dramatic final value.

title: Dehydration To Shock
summary: A toddler has vomiting and diarrhea. Trend the vitals and identify the earliest deterioration that should change urgency.
etiology_question: What is the most likely pattern?
response_question: What is the best immediate EMS response?

response_choices.label: Treat as shock, reassess perfusion, and expedite transport
Wait for urine output before changing urgency
Focus on oral intake challenge before transport

feedback.timeline: The trend becomes urgent when tachycardia rises while blood pressure begins dropping.
feedback.etiology: Vomiting/diarrhea plus worsening tachycardia and falling systolic pressure fits hypovolemic shock.
feedback.response: Treat as shock and expedite transport rather than waiting for late signs.

hint: Look for the first point where more than one channel starts moving in the wrong direction, not just the most dramatic final value.
